name.text: M3Q01: The sf package in R implements the:

questiontext.text: The sf package in R implements the:

generalfeedback.text: The sf (Simple Features) package provides an R implementation of the ISO 19125 Simple Features standard. It represents spatial data as data frames with a special geometry column, enabling seamless integration with tidyverse workflows.

answer.text: Simple Features standard for representing vector spatial data

feedback.text: Correct! The sf (Simple Features) package provides an R implementation of the ISO 19125 Simple Features standard. It represents spatial data as data frames with a special geometry column, enabling seamless integration with tidyverse workflows.

Incorrect. The sf (Simple Features) package provides an R implementation of the ISO 19125 Simple Features standard. It represents spatial data as data frames with a special geometry column, enabling seamless integration with tidyverse workflows.

Incorrect. The sf (Simple Features) package provides an R implementation of the ISO 19125 Simple Features standard. It represents spatial data as data frames with a special geometry column, enabling seamless integration with tidyverse workflows.

answer.text: Time-series analysis framework for temporal health data

feedback.text: Incorrect. The sf (Simple Features) package provides an R implementation of the ISO 19125 Simple Features standard. It represents spatial data as data frames with a special geometry column, enabling seamless integration with tidyverse workflows.

name.text: M3Q02: An sf object in R differs from a regular data frame because it:

questiontext.text: An sf object in R differs from a regular data frame because it:

generalfeedback.text: An sf object is a data.frame with a geometry list-column. This design means ALL dplyr verbs (filter, mutate, group_by, summarize, joins) work directly on sf objects, making spatial analysis seamlessly integrated with data wrangling.

answer.text: Contains a special geometry column (sfc) that stores spatial information alongside regular attribute columns

feedback.text: Correct! An sf object is a data.frame with a geometry list-column. This design means ALL dplyr verbs (filter, mutate, group_by, summarize, joins) work directly on sf objects, making spatial analysis seamlessly integrated with data wrangling.

answer.text: Can only store point data, not polygon data

feedback.text: Incorrect. An sf object is a data.frame with a geometry list-column. This design means ALL dplyr verbs (filter, mutate, group_by, summarize, joins) work directly on sf objects, making spatial analysis seamlessly integrated with data wrangling.

answer.text: Cannot be manipulated with dplyr verbs like filter() and mutate()

feedback.text: Incorrect. An sf object is a data.frame with a geometry list-column. This design means ALL dplyr verbs (filter, mutate, group_by, summarize, joins) work directly on sf objects, making spatial analysis seamlessly integrated with data wrangling.

answer.text: Requires a separate file to store the coordinate reference system

feedback.text: Incorrect. An sf object is a data.frame with a geometry list-column. This design means ALL dplyr verbs (filter, mutate, group_by, summarize, joins) work directly on sf objects, making spatial analysis seamlessly integrated with data wrangling.

name.text: M3Q03: To load an ESRI Shapefile into R as an sf object, you use:

questiontext.text: To load an ESRI Shapefile into R as an sf object, you use:

generalfeedback.text: st_read() reads spatial vector data from files (shapefiles, GeoJSON, GeoPackage, etc.). st_as_sf() converts existing R objects (e.g., data frames with lat/lon columns) to sf objects. read.csv() cannot handle spatial files.

feedback.text: Correct! st_read() reads spatial vector data from files (shapefiles, GeoJSON, GeoPackage, etc.). st_as_sf() converts existing R objects (e.g., data frames with lat/lon columns) to sf objects. read.csv() cannot handle spatial files.

Incorrect. st_read() reads spatial vector data from files (shapefiles, GeoJSON, GeoPackage, etc.). st_as_sf() converts existing R objects (e.g., data frames with lat/lon columns) to sf objects. read.csv() cannot handle spatial files.

Incorrect. st_read() reads spatial vector data from files (shapefiles, GeoJSON, GeoPackage, etc.). st_as_sf() converts existing R objects (e.g., data frames with lat/lon columns) to sf objects. read.csv() cannot handle spatial files.

Incorrect. st_read() reads spatial vector data from files (shapefiles, GeoJSON, GeoPackage, etc.). st_as_sf() converts existing R objects (e.g., data frames with lat/lon columns) to sf objects. read.csv() cannot handle spatial files.

name.text: M3Q04: To check the coordinate reference system of an sf object called ‘india_sf’, you

questiontext.text: To check the coordinate reference system of an sf object called ‘india_sf’, you use:

generalfeedback.text: st_crs() returns the CRS of an sf object, including the EPSG code and PROJ string. This is always the first check after loading spatial data to ensure the CRS is correct before analysis.

feedback.text: Correct! st_crs() returns the CRS of an sf object, including the EPSG code and PROJ string. This is always the first check after loading spatial data to ensure the CRS is correct before analysis.
Incorrect. st_crs() returns the CRS of an sf object, including the EPSG code and PROJ string. This is always the first check after loading spatial data to ensure the CRS is correct before analysis.
Incorrect. st_crs() returns the CRS of an sf object, including the EPSG code and PROJ string. This is always the first check after loading spatial data to ensure the CRS is correct before analysis.
Incorrect. st_crs() returns the CRS of an sf object, including the EPSG code and PROJ string. This is always the first check after loading spatial data to ensure the CRS is correct before analysis.

name.text: M3Q05: To transform an sf object from EPSG:4326 to EPSG:32643, you use:

questiontext.text: To transform an sf object from EPSG:4326 to EPSG:32643, you use:

generalfeedback.text: st_transform() reprojects spatial data from one CRS to another, performing the mathematical transformation of coordinates. st_set_crs() only LABELS the CRS without transforming coordinates (dangerous if used incorrectly). st_crs() only queries the CRS.

feedback.text: Correct! st_transform() reprojects spatial data from one CRS to another, performing the mathematical transformation of coordinates. st_set_crs() only LABELS the CRS without transforming coordinates (dangerous if used incorrectly). st_crs() only queries the CRS.

Incorrect. st_transform() reprojects spatial data from one CRS to another, performing the mathematical transformation of coordinates. st_set_crs() only LABELS the CRS without transforming coordinates (dangerous if used incorrectly). st_crs() only queries the CRS.

Incorrect. st_transform() reprojects spatial data from one CRS to another, performing the mathematical transformation of coordinates. st_set_crs() only LABELS the CRS without transforming coordinates (dangerous if used incorrectly). st_crs() only queries the CRS.

Incorrect. st_transform() reprojects spatial data from one CRS to another, performing the mathematical transformation of coordinates. st_set_crs() only LABELS the CRS without transforming coordinates (dangerous if used incorrectly). st_crs() only queries the CRS.

name.text: M3Q06: When joining a non-spatial data frame to an sf object using left_join(), why is

questiontext.text: When joining a non-spatial data frame to an sf object using left_join(), why is name harmonization often necessary?

generalfeedback.text: Name harmonization is one of the most time-consuming but critical steps in spatial data science. Indian state names are particularly challenging: Chattisgarh/Chhattisgarh, Pondicherry/Puducherry, Orissa/Odisha, etc. case_when() is the standard tool.

answer.text: Because the same geographic unit may be spelled differently across datasets (e.g., ‘Orissa’ vs ‘Odisha’)

feedback.text: Correct! Name harmonization is one of the most time-consuming but critical steps in spatial data science. Indian state names are particularly challenging: Chattisgarh/Chhattisgarh, Pondicherry/Puducherry, Orissa/Odisha, etc. case_when() is the standard tool.

Incorrect. Name harmonization is one of the most time-consuming but critical steps in spatial data science. Indian state names are particularly challenging: Chattisgarh/Chhattisgarh, Pondicherry/Puducherry, Orissa/Odisha, etc. case_when() is the standard tool.

answer.text: Because spatial and non-spatial data cannot share column names

feedback.text: Incorrect. Name harmonization is one of the most time-consuming but critical steps in spatial data science. Indian state names are particularly challenging: Chattisgarh/Chhattisgarh, Pondicherry/Puducherry, Orissa/Odisha, etc. case_when() is the standard tool.

answer.text: Because R automatically corrects all spelling differences

feedback.text: Incorrect. Name harmonization is one of the most time-consuming but critical steps in spatial data science. Indian state names are particularly challenging: Chattisgarh/Chhattisgarh, Pondicherry/Puducherry, Orissa/Odisha, etc. case_when() is the standard tool.

name.text: M3Q07: The function exactextractr::exact_extract(raster, polygons, fun = ‘sum’) is used

questiontext.text: The function exactextractr::exact_extract(raster, polygons, fun = ‘sum’) is used to:

generalfeedback.text: exact_extract() performs zonal statistics: it extracts raster values within each polygon and applies a summary function (sum, mean, max, etc.). This is how we calculate population per state from a gridded population raster.

answer.text: Calculate the sum of raster cell values that fall within each polygon (zonal statistics)

feedback.text: Correct! exact_extract() performs zonal statistics: it extracts raster values within each polygon and applies a summary function (sum, mean, max, etc.). This is how we calculate population per state from a gridded population raster.

answer.text: Extract the CRS from a raster file

feedback.text: Incorrect. exact_extract() performs zonal statistics: it extracts raster values within each polygon and applies a summary function (sum, mean, max, etc.). This is how we calculate population per state from a gridded population raster.

answer.text: Convert a raster to a vector polygon

feedback.text: Incorrect. exact_extract() performs zonal statistics: it extracts raster values within each polygon and applies a summary function (sum, mean, max, etc.). This is how we calculate population per state from a gridded population raster.

Incorrect. exact_extract() performs zonal statistics: it extracts raster values within each polygon and applies a summary function (sum, mean, max, etc.). This is how we calculate population per state from a gridded population raster.

name.text: M3Q08: To geocode addresses (convert text addresses to latitude/longitude coordinates)

questiontext.text: To geocode addresses (convert text addresses to latitude/longitude coordinates) using an open-source method in R, you use:

generalfeedback.text: tidygeocoder provides a tidy interface to multiple geocoding services. The OSM (OpenStreetMap/Nominatim) method is free and requires no API key. ggmap’s Google method requires a paid API key.

feedback.text: Correct! tidygeocoder provides a tidy interface to multiple geocoding services. The OSM (OpenStreetMap/Nominatim) method is free and requires no API key. ggmap’s Google method requires a paid API key.

Incorrect. tidygeocoder provides a tidy interface to multiple geocoding services. The OSM (OpenStreetMap/Nominatim) method is free and requires no API key. ggmap’s Google method requires a paid API key.

Incorrect. tidygeocoder provides a tidy interface to multiple geocoding services. The OSM (OpenStreetMap/Nominatim) method is free and requires no API key. ggmap’s Google method requires a paid API key.

Incorrect. tidygeocoder provides a tidy interface to multiple geocoding services. The OSM (OpenStreetMap/Nominatim) method is free and requires no API key. ggmap’s Google method requires a paid API key.

name.text: M3Q09: To convert a data frame with columns ‘long’ and ‘lat’ into an sf POINT object wi

questiontext.text: To convert a data frame with columns ‘long’ and ‘lat’ into an sf POINT object with WGS 84 CRS, you use:

generalfeedback.text: st_as_sf() with coords = c(‘long’, ‘lat’) converts tabular data to spatial. CRITICAL: the order is c(X, Y) = c(longitude, latitude). Reversing them (lat, long) places points in the wrong location — a very common error.

feedback.text: Correct! st_as_sf() with coords = c(‘long’, ‘lat’) converts tabular data to spatial. CRITICAL: the order is c(X, Y) = c(longitude, latitude). Reversing them (lat, long) places points in the wrong location — a very common error.

Incorrect. st_as_sf() with coords = c(‘long’, ‘lat’) converts tabular data to spatial. CRITICAL: the order is c(X, Y) = c(longitude, latitude). Reversing them (lat, long) places points in the wrong location — a very common error.

Incorrect. st_as_sf() with coords = c(‘long’, ‘lat’) converts tabular data to spatial. CRITICAL: the order is c(X, Y) = c(longitude, latitude). Reversing them (lat, long) places points in the wrong location — a very common error.

Incorrect. st_as_sf() with coords = c(‘long’, ‘lat’) converts tabular data to spatial. CRITICAL: the order is c(X, Y) = c(longitude, latitude). Reversing them (lat, long) places points in the wrong location — a very common error.

name.text: M3Q10: To create a choropleth map of ‘no_of_colleges’ in ggplot2, the correct geom_sf()

questiontext.text: To create a choropleth map of ‘no_of_colleges’ in ggplot2, the correct geom_sf() call is:

generalfeedback.text: For choropleths, map the variable to the fill aesthetic inside aes(). The color aesthetic controls the border color of polygons. Using geom_sf() (not geom_polygon()) is essential because it handles sf geometry automatically.

feedback.text: Correct! For choropleths, map the variable to the fill aesthetic inside aes(). The color aesthetic controls the border color of polygons. Using geom_sf() (not geom_polygon()) is essential because it handles sf geometry automatically.

Incorrect. For choropleths, map the variable to the fill aesthetic inside aes(). The color aesthetic controls the border color of polygons. Using geom_sf() (not geom_polygon()) is essential because it handles sf geometry automatically.

Incorrect. For choropleths, map the variable to the fill aesthetic inside aes(). The color aesthetic controls the border color of polygons. Using geom_sf() (not geom_polygon()) is essential because it handles sf geometry automatically.

Incorrect. For choropleths, map the variable to the fill aesthetic inside aes(). The color aesthetic controls the border color of polygons. Using geom_sf() (not geom_polygon()) is essential because it handles sf geometry automatically.

name.text: M3Q11: The scale_fill_viridis_c() palette is recommended for choropleth maps because it

questiontext.text: The scale_fill_viridis_c() palette is recommended for choropleth maps because it is:

generalfeedback.text: The viridis family of palettes was designed to be perceptually uniform (equal steps in data = equal steps in perceived color), accessible to people with color vision deficiencies, and readable when printed in black and white.

answer.text: Perceptually uniform, colorblind-friendly, and prints well in grayscale

feedback.text: Correct! The viridis family of palettes was designed to be perceptually uniform (equal steps in data = equal steps in perceived color), accessible to people with color vision deficiencies, and readable when printed in black and white.

answer.text: The only color palette available in ggplot2

feedback.text: Incorrect. The viridis family of palettes was designed to be perceptually uniform (equal steps in data = equal steps in perceived color), accessible to people with color vision deficiencies, and readable when printed in black and white.

Incorrect. The viridis family of palettes was designed to be perceptually uniform (equal steps in data = equal steps in perceived color), accessible to people with color vision deficiencies, and readable when printed in black and white.

Incorrect. The viridis family of palettes was designed to be perceptually uniform (equal steps in data = equal steps in perceived color), accessible to people with color vision deficiencies, and readable when printed in black and white.

name.text: M3Q12: To add a scale bar and north arrow to a ggplot2 map, you use functions from the:

questiontext.text: To add a scale bar and north arrow to a ggplot2 map, you use functions from the:

generalfeedback.text: ggspatial extends ggplot2 with spatial-specific map elements. annotation_scale() adds a scale bar and annotation_north_arrow() adds a north arrow. Both accept location parameters (e.g., ‘bl’ for bottom-left, ‘tr’ for top-right).

feedback.text: Correct! ggspatial extends ggplot2 with spatial-specific map elements. annotation_scale() adds a scale bar and annotation_north_arrow() adds a north arrow. Both accept location parameters (e.g., ‘bl’ for bottom-left, ‘tr’ for top-right).

Incorrect. ggspatial extends ggplot2 with spatial-specific map elements. annotation_scale() adds a scale bar and annotation_north_arrow() adds a north arrow. Both accept location parameters (e.g., ‘bl’ for bottom-left, ‘tr’ for top-right).

Incorrect. ggspatial extends ggplot2 with spatial-specific map elements. annotation_scale() adds a scale bar and annotation_north_arrow() adds a north arrow. Both accept location parameters (e.g., ‘bl’ for bottom-left, ‘tr’ for top-right).

Incorrect. ggspatial extends ggplot2 with spatial-specific map elements. annotation_scale() adds a scale bar and annotation_north_arrow() adds a north arrow. Both accept location parameters (e.g., ‘bl’ for bottom-left, ‘tr’ for top-right).

name.text: M3Q13: The raster::crop() function clips a raster to the geographic extent (bounding bo

questiontext.text: The raster::crop() function clips a raster to the geographic extent (bounding box) of a spatial object.

generalfeedback.text: crop() trims the raster to the bounding box of the provided extent or spatial object. This reduces computation time when you only need raster data for a specific region (e.g., cropping a global population raster to India).

feedback.text: Correct! crop() trims the raster to the bounding box of the provided extent or spatial object. This reduces computation time when you only need raster data for a specific region (e.g., cropping a global population raster to India).

Incorrect. crop() trims the raster to the bounding box of the provided extent or spatial object. This reduces computation time when you only need raster data for a specific region (e.g., cropping a global population raster to India).

name.text: M3Q14: The sf package depends on which external system libraries?

questiontext.text: The sf package depends on which external system libraries?

generalfeedback.text: sf links to three core geospatial C/C++ libraries: GDAL (reading/writing 200+ spatial formats), GEOS (geometric operations like buffering, intersection), and PROJ (coordinate reference system transformations). These must be installed at the system level.

answer.text: GDAL (data I/O), GEOS (geometry operations), and PROJ (coordinate transformations)

feedback.text: Correct! sf links to three core geospatial C/C++ libraries: GDAL (reading/writing 200+ spatial formats), GEOS (geometric operations like buffering, intersection), and PROJ (coordinate reference system transformations). These must be installed at the system level.

Incorrect. sf links to three core geospatial C/C++ libraries: GDAL (reading/writing 200+ spatial formats), GEOS (geometric operations like buffering, intersection), and PROJ (coordinate reference system transformations). These must be installed at the system level.

Incorrect. sf links to three core geospatial C/C++ libraries: GDAL (reading/writing 200+ spatial formats), GEOS (geometric operations like buffering, intersection), and PROJ (coordinate reference system transformations). These must be installed at the system level.

Incorrect. sf links to three core geospatial C/C++ libraries: GDAL (reading/writing 200+ spatial formats), GEOS (geometric operations like buffering, intersection), and PROJ (coordinate reference system transformations). These must be installed at the system level.

name.text: M3Q15: To display two maps side by side with a shared legend in R, you use the patchwor

questiontext.text: To display two maps side by side with a shared legend in R, you use the patchwork package like this:

generalfeedback.text: patchwork’s plot_layout(guides = ‘collect’) merges identical legends from multiple plots into a single shared legend. Combine with & scale_fill_*() to ensure both plots use the same color scale with matched limits.

feedback.text: Correct! patchwork’s plot_layout(guides = ‘collect’) merges identical legends from multiple plots into a single shared legend. Combine with & scale_fill_*() to ensure both plots use the same color scale with matched limits.

Incorrect. patchwork’s plot_layout(guides = ‘collect’) merges identical legends from multiple plots into a single shared legend. Combine with & scale_fill_*() to ensure both plots use the same color scale with matched limits.

Incorrect. patchwork’s plot_layout(guides = ‘collect’) merges identical legends from multiple plots into a single shared legend. Combine with & scale_fill_*() to ensure both plots use the same color scale with matched limits.

Incorrect. patchwork’s plot_layout(guides = ‘collect’) merges identical legends from multiple plots into a single shared legend. Combine with & scale_fill_*() to ensure both plots use the same color scale with matched limits.
